list.text: https://www.desmos.com/calculator/peztuuwuqi

I've always found parabolas interesting. Something about the way that a function as simple as x^2 is so useful. For example, it matches the trajectory of an object without air friction, and is also the set of points equidistant to a point and a line. Isn't that just so poetic, in the way that math is?
After deciding that I would only use parabolas, I then found some nice reference images online with cute looking foxes. I decided to take inspiration from them and made a simple sketch.
A basic sketch that I made in GIMP, as a starting point to trace off of.

Inspiration:                            
http://tinyurl.com/3a29y43h    http://tinyurl.com/yc7rh3kn
So I decided to mix the two, and overlap multiple parabolas (without any other functions) to create a cute little fox!
And so my idea came to fruition - Enjoy this fox made from parabolas and only parabolas!
This is the animation controller for the tail.
This is the animation controller for the background (not recommended).
Some functions that helped streamline the creation of this fox.
Below is a parabola function, that I used for all of my parabolas in this graph.
x and y are the two input coordinates, h and k are the cartesian coordinates (before rotation is applied), a is the width of the parabola, and theta is the rotation value.
These two are some simple rotation functions
This is a simple function that I used to simplify these animations.
It turns every input value into a parabola, so that even my animations are parabolic. v is the input value, o is the offset of the animation, and m is the width of the parabola.
Finally, this is my "phase modulation" function that changes a value from -1 to 1 by a set amount, but bring the value back to -1 if it exceeded 1.
I used this to offset all of the background parabolas' bouncing so that they all form a neat, looping animation.
These are the parameters of the animations. t_o and t_m are the offset and multiplier for the tail animation, and b_go and b_gm are the same but for the background animation.
These are all the custom colors that I used.
The background fill and the parabolas.
Required absolute function in order to fill the background :/
This is the "base" of the fox - it contains all of the main parts of the fox, without details or lighting.
These are the shadows on more darker shaded areas of the fox.
These are the highlights on more lightly shaded areas.
These are the fine details of the fox - the eyes and nose.
